title: Carduus horridissimus
taxonomy: Carduus horridissimus is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae. It is a member of the Carduus genus, commonly known as plums or thistles. The plant's classification is further divided into the kingdom Plantae and the phylum Magnoliophyta. This classification places Carduus horridissimus within the broader group of dicotyledonous plants. The exact classification may vary depending on the source and specific taxonomic system used.
uses: Carduus horridissimus, also known as the spiny plumeless thistle, has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties. Its leaves and flowers are said to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which may be used to treat wounds and skin conditions. The plant's extracts have also been used to treat digestive issues and reduce fever. In some folk remedies, the plant is used to treat respiratory problems, such as bronchitis and asthma. However, more research is needed to confirm the effectiveness of these uses.

preparations: Carduus horridissimus, also known as the giant thistle, is a plant used in traditional herbal medicine. Its spiny stems and leaves have been used to create infusions and teas, said to have anti-inflammatory properties. Some herbal preparations using this plant claim to aid in digestion and relieve symptoms of indigestion. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy of these uses. The plant's spiny nature also makes it difficult to handle, requiring caution when processing it into herbal preparations.
side_effects: Carduus horridissimus, also known as the giant thistle, contains cardenolides which are toxic to humans and animals. Ingestion of the plant can cause nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. The cardenolides in Carduus horridissimus can also affect the heart and nervous system, leading to more severe symptoms such as arrhythmias and seizures. If ingested in large amounts, it can be fatal. It is essential to handle the plant with caution and avoid any potential exposure.
